focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

The first thing the airport apartment taught Tiana was that air had weight.

Every few minutes a plane came down low enough to make the blinds tremble in their tracks. The building sat behind a tire shop and beside a chain-link lot full of white service vans with company names half-peeled off their sides. Jet fuel, hot rubber, old frying oil from somewhere downstairs: the smell outside did not sort itself into separate things. It pressed in together. Even the stairwell felt used up before anybody climbed it.

Marcia stood at the narrow kitchen counter with her purse tucked under one arm and signed where the leasing woman tapped. The counter was so shallow that her elbow kept knocking the wall. A plastic grocery bag with two rolls of shelf paper and a bottle of bleach hung from her wrist like she had come to clean instead of to claim. Maybe both were true.

"Initial there too," the leasing woman said. She spoke around a peppermint. Her thumbnail had a blue evil-eye painted on it, chipped at the center. "And that's acknowledging the parking rules."

Marcia read the line before she wrote anything. She always did that. Tiana had spent half her life watching her mother scan a sentence like it had teeth hidden in it.

"One assigned space," Marcia said. "We don't have a car."

"Then nobody can tow you." The woman gave a small shrug. "That part is easier."

The apartment was smaller than the garage had been. The garage had been poor in the open, one long room pretending a curtain and a kitchenette could divide a life into proper pieces. This place was poor by narrowing. A short hall, a bedroom barely big enough for a full mattress, a bathroom with a swollen door, a living room that already looked occupied by the air conditioner and the noise. There were no rooster...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...all, mouth open, face older than she had wanted to admit even in anger. Micah was on his phone under his towel, one leg bouncing. Kerene had somehow made a donated oversized T-shirt look intentional and was helping an elderly woman on the next cot untangle a chain from her hair scarf while talking low and fast enough to keep the woman laughing. Tiana was still at the table with the papers, reading every line as if force of attention alone could flatten them dry.

Marcia set her plate down untouched for a moment and pressed the partner notebook open with both hands.

The fan lifted the corners of the wet pages. They kept trying to curl back into themselves. She flattened them again. If she let go, the pages would buckle. If she pressed too hard, they would tear. It was delicate, annoying work, the kind no disaster movie respected because there was nothing grand in it. Still it was the work the day had left her.

Above the murmur of the hall, Sister Eudora was somewhere in the kitchen scolding someone for putting wet towels on a polished pew. Deacon Lewis laughed at something too tired to be funny. A child asked for their mother in that lost, casual way children did when they assumed the answer would come. Marcia kept one hand on the notebook and one on Tiana's folder so the fan would not carry either off the table.

By night the fellowship hall had become its own weather. Snoring, muttering, phone light on faces, the hiss of the industrial fan, toilets flushing without shame, coughs stitched from one end of the room to the other. Marcia sat awake in the plastic chair by the papers because she could not trust sleep with so much of their life laid out drying in public.

The ink kept drying lighter than it had been written. The pages kept holding the waterline.

So did she.
